claims.evidenceExcerpt: In order to really unlock async, we needed longer task horizons. And so we're starting to see that now.
claims.text: Martin argues that async agents only become practical once models can sustain much longer autonomous work than the old chat-and-local-coding regime.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: We put the harness in the sandbox in the same container. Now the problem here is, what happens if the harness dies or the container dies?
claims.text: He reports that putting the harness and the sandbox in the same container makes long-horizon agents brittle because losing the container can lose the session.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: So, for example, giving Claude access to a bunch of your secrets and letting it run for 10 hours and not watching it can be a little bit spooky and have some security concerns, especially as models get extremely capable.
claims.text: He says sharing secrets with a long-running execution container is a security concern as models become more capable.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: They're stored in a separate vault. So, this decoupling actually makes it quite reliable and safe, particularly for long-horizon tasks.
claims.text: He argues that decoupling the brain from the hands, with a stateless harness, append-only session, and separate vault, improves reliability and safety.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: And so, what we found is it's quite effective to separate verification into a separate context window.
claims.text: He says verification should be separated into its own context window because self-grading in the same context can produce confabulation and odd artifacts.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: What you see is the frontier capability models are extremely good with this pattern of kind of loops in software and and kind of verification because what happens is instead of encoding steering me and into like me as the human, you're encoding the signal into the environment.
claims.text: He reports that looped verifier setups let frontier models self-correct by encoding the signal in the environment rather than relying on human steering.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: So, the key point I'm making here is that Claude has gotten much better at this in-band memory writing across model generations.
claims.text: He says Claude has gotten much better at in-band memory writing across model generations.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: With the dreaming, this error is corrected, and it's able to properly localize itself and not fall fall down this trap.
claims.text: He reports that offline dreaming can correct an incorrect memory and prevent the repeated failure it causes in the task.

payload.methodNotes: Each non-summary entry is anchored to exactly one transcript segment id. I chose the most direct segment for each concept and kept the synthesis limited to what the speaker explicitly said or strongly implied in the talk.

methods.description: Keep the harness stateless and move durable state into an append-only session log.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: So, the story here is that the harness becomes a stateless process that talks to a session.
methods.name: Append-only session log

methods.description: Grade work in a separate verifier context instead of in the same context that produced it.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: And so, what we found is it's quite effective to separate verification into a separate context window.
methods.name: Separate verifier context

methods.description: Run a build context and a verifier context in a loop until the verifier accepts the outcome.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: [snorts] And so, the way this works in practice is when you build loops, you can have a loop of a build context and a verifier context.
methods.name: Build-verifier loop

methods.description: Let the model manage memory through a general substrate rather than a prescriptive schema.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: So, I think what we've seen is that very general substrates for memory, be it just your database or file system, are good because the model can manage them freely versus a very very kind of like prescriptive memory schema they are trying to pigeonhole the model into.
methods.name: General memory substrate

methods.description: Use an offline dreaming pass to inspect traces and repair bad memories.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: This is a very important nuance in this process of dreaming is is kind of an offline or out of band process that we've used to consolidate and improve memory.
methods.name: Offline memory dreaming

methods.description: Share one harness across many users with organizational identity and context.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: So, it is a multiplayer harness. And what's nice about that is it has its own identity. Its identity and credentials are not tied to a given user and has access to organizational level context, not just my local context.
methods.name: Multiplayer org harness

questions.evidenceExcerpt: So, I think what we've seen is that very general substrates for memory, be it just your database or file system, are good because the model can manage them freely versus a very very kind of like prescriptive memory schema they are trying to pigeonhole the model into.
questions.question: What memory substrate best supports model-managed memory when the structure stays highly programmable instead of prescriptive?
questions.whyItMatters: The talk suggests memory design matters as much as model capability, but leaves open how to choose the best general-purpose substrate.

questions.evidenceExcerpt: The key point is that we've actually run a lot of different evals showing that dreaming can indeed improve performance for very intuitive reasons as you see here.
questions.question: How should offline dreaming be evaluated to tell whether the memory corrections are actually worth the extra compute?
questions.whyItMatters: If dreaming is going to be part of an agent harness, teams need a reliable way to measure whether it improves real task performance.

questions.evidenceExcerpt: So, actually I think to build real agents that can operate in these long time horizons, a bunch of things need to come together in terms of like architecture, infrastructure, security, memory.
questions.question: What combination of model capability, memory, security, and architecture is required to sustain the long-horizon gap for real agents?
questions.whyItMatters: The talk argues that long-horizon performance is not just a model issue, so the open question is which system components matter most.

payload.summary: Martin argues that long-horizon AI work is no longer primarily a prompt-design problem; it is a harness-design problem, because models are now capable of hours of autonomous work and need infrastructure that matches that horizon. His main prescription is to separate the model's reasoning process from execution, verification, and memory: keep the harness stateless, keep session state append-only, use independent verifier contexts, and let the model write to general memory substrates that can later be consolidated offline. He also shows that these patterns matter in practice: looped verification can drive strong benchmark performance, in-band memory writing gets better as model capacity rises, and offline dreaming can repair bad memories that would otherwise cause repeated failures. The practical consequence is a shift from single-user, reactive agents to shared, proactive org-level harnesses that are more reliable, safer with credentials, and easier for teams to use.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: They're stored in a separate vault. So, this decoupling actually makes it quite reliable and safe, particularly for long-horizon tasks.
takeaways.text: Treat the harness as stateless and the session as durable if you want long-horizon work to survive failures cleanly.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Um and the reason is the verifier context can be tuned very specifically for the critical verification task.
takeaways.text: Do not use the same context to do work and judge it; separate verification context improves reliability.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Let the model structure and maintain its own memory. Don't give it a prescribed memory schema.
takeaways.text: Let the model structure and maintain its own memory instead of forcing a fixed memory schema.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Because what it does is it looks at your memory store and it looks at all your prior traces or sessions and kind of can find and correct errors.
takeaways.text: Use offline consolidation to inspect memory traces and correct mistakes that would otherwise persist.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Um and this is a very important kind of new kind of UX that I think is going to be more and more common with async agents that kind of access to organizational context.
takeaways.text: Build async agents that can proactively alert users based on shared organizational context, not just react to prompts.

tools.description: The prompt-response API the speaker describes as a simple base for building harnesses.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: So if you look at the lower left, Messages API came out like 2 years ago. It's basically prompt response.
tools.name: Messages API

tools.description: The programmatic surface for calling Claude Code as a harness.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: Now over the past year, we saw the rise of, you know, coding agents in particular. So we released Agent SDK.
tools.name: Agent SDK

tools.description: The packaged API surface that includes both the harness and managed deployment infrastructure.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: But over the past few months, since April, as we've seen longer longer task horizons, we released a new API called Managed Agents, which basically packages both the harness as well as all the managed deployment infrastructure for you.
tools.name: Managed Agents

tools.description: The synchronous coding agent the speaker cites as a shift toward about an hour of work.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: Now, the past year we saw the rise of synchronous coding agents like Claude code. And this is, you know, a kind of a shift because then models could do maybe an hour of work.
tools.name: Claude Code

tools.description: The org-level harness the speaker uses as an example of multiplayer async agents.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: so, we released Claude Tag and a lot of the reaction was like, "Ah, Slack bot." And like, look, I have actually created a lot of Slack bots myself.
tools.name: Claude Tag

tools.description: The earlier model used in the Pokémon memory-writing example.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: And here's the key point. When Sonnet 3.5 is given this access to a memory directory, and it can write memory, {quote} in-band, as it progresses through this game, it's not very good.
tools.name: Sonnet 3.5

tools.description: The newer model used in the memory-writing and continual-learning comparisons.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: It's it's not very strategic. And the game progress is quite limited. But with more recent models like this is looking at 4 6, the notes are much more strategic and game progress is much further.

topics.description: Agents that can run for hours with limited human steering and need durable orchestration.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: In order to really unlock async, we needed longer task horizons. And so we're starting to see that now.
topics.name: Long-horizon async agents

topics.description: Architectures that separate reasoning and coordination from execution environments.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: So, for this reason, we kind of decouple what we call the brain, that's the harness, from the hands, the execution environments, and manage agents to set up like this.
topics.name: Brain-hand decoupling

topics.description: Using an independent context to judge work rather than self-grading in the same context.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: And this continues in a loop until verification is complete. And this is really the big idea behind this whole loops trend that you might have heard about and we found it to be a kind of a very powerful paradigm especially for working with some of the higher capacity models.
topics.name: Verifier loops

topics.description: Writing memory during execution through simple writable storage primitives.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: So, this is showing Claude's capacity as an in-band memory writer. So, you basically give Claude memory tools.
topics.name: In-band memory writing

topics.description: Offline passes that inspect traces and repair memory errors.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: This is a very important nuance in this process of dreaming is is kind of an offline or out of band process that we've used to consolidate and improve memory.
topics.name: Dreaming-based memory consolidation

topics.description: Shared harnesses with organizational identity, organizational context, and team-wide access.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: What's interesting about Claude Tag is it is a harness that everyone in the organization has access to and can use.
topics.name: Org-level harnesses

topics.description: Agents that can alert users based on shared context rather than only responding to direct prompts.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: They're responsive to how you steer it. versus async agents increasingly have the ability to steer proactivity.
topics.name: Proactive agent UX
